Download
For macOS 14.6 and later · Public Beta available

Close the window.
Quit the app.

On macOS, closing the last window doesn't actually quit the app. Safari, Notes, Preview, and dozens more keep running in the background. Nix notices and cleanly quits them — the way you expected all along.

Perfect for developers, designers, students, and anyone who prefers apps to actually quit when they're closed.

Download for macOSSee how it works
7-day free trial · Native SwiftUI · No usage tracking · 2.4 MB · $9.99
The problem

Closing a window isn't the same as quitting an app.

For apps like Music or Messages, background running is intentional and useful. For everything else, it just drains memory and clutters your dock. Nix gives you the control to decide which apps actually quit.

Without Nix
Seven apps still running. You closed every window an hour ago.
S
N
P
M
M
M
P
Active background apps7
With Nix
You closed the windows. The apps are gone. Your Mac feels lighter.
S
N
P
M
M
M
P
Active background apps1
How it works

Three steps. Invisible to you.

Nix runs quietly in the menu bar. It only acts when you do.

01

Detect

Nix observes window lifecycle events through the macOS Accessibility API. The moment an app's last window closes, Nix knows.

AXObserver · Event-driven · Zero polling
02

Decide

Each app gets its own rule. Quit, Hide, Ignore, or Prompt. Add a grace period in case a new window opens within seconds.

Per-app rules · Configurable grace
03

Act

Nix sends a clean termination — the same as ⌘Q. Apps handle their own shutdown safely. Nothing is force-killed.

Native termination · Safe by design
Features

Small app.
Big control.

Automatic Quitting

When the last window closes, Nix gracefully quits the app. Just like ⌘Q.

Per-App Rules

Quit, Hide, Ignore, or Prompt — set the behavior for every app individually.

Grace Period

Configurable delay before quitting, in case a new window opens.

Whitelist

Keep specific apps running forever. Background daemons stay untouched.

Pause Mode

One click pauses all monitoring. Resume whenever.

Privacy First

No analytics. No usage tracking. One network call for license validation only.

Launch at Login

Install once. Nix runs quietly in the menu bar from boot.

Native Performance

Written entirely in Swift. Event-driven. Near-zero CPU usage.

Why Nix

The most flexible quit-on-close experience for macOS.

Native SwiftUI UI

Launch at Login

No Usage Tracking
Varies

Nix combines powerful automation, native macOS design, and flexible app-specific control in a single lightweight utility.

One-Time Purchase · No subscriptions ever
Built for macOS

Native, all the way down.

No Electron. No web layer. No background polling. Just Swift, the Accessibility API, and event-driven observers — exactly how a Mac utility should be built.

SwiftSwiftUIAppKitAccessibility APISMAppServiceAXObserver
WindowMonitor.swift
// Listen for the last window close — no polling.
import ApplicationServices

final class WindowMonitor {
  private let observer: AXObserver

  func onLastWindowClosed(app: NSRunningApplication) {
    // Apply per-app rule: Quit · Hide · Ignore · Prompt
    let rule = RuleStore.rule(for: app.bundleIdentifier)
    switch rule {
      case .quit:    app.terminate()
      case .hide:    app.hide()
      case .ignore:  /* no-op */
      case .prompt:  NixPrompt.ask(for: app)
    }
  }
}

Your usage stays
entirely yours.

Nix has no analytics SDK, no crash reporter, and no tracking of any kind. The only network activity is a single license check against Lemon Squeezy when you activate — nothing else Nix does ever touches the network.

No analytics
No usage tracking
One network call (license only)
No Nix-hosted accounts
Loved by Mac users

Quietly indispensable.
